comments.text: "depending on $c_1$" should of course be "depending on $c_2$". (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
It seems likely that one could deduce this from a sufficiently uniform version of the prime tuples conjecture.

Indeed, looks like the following argument would work: Let $c_1,c_2>0$ and put $c=\lfloor c_2\rfloor$. We seek a block of $>c_1\log x$ consecutive primes $\le x$ whose adjacent gaps all exceed $c$. A natural conditional route is to assume a short-interval, uniformly averaged Hardy--Littlewood $k$-tuple conjecture at the scale $T\asymp(\log x)^2$: for each fixed $m\ge1$, uniformly over intervals $I=[u,u+T]\subset[x/2,x]$ and distinct shifts $H=\{h_1,\dots,h_m\}\subset[0,T+c]$,\[ \#\{n\in I:\ n+h_i\ \text{prime for all }i\} = (1+o(1))\,\mathfrak S(H)\,\frac{T}{(\log x)^m},\]as $x\to \infty$, with $o(1)$ uniform in $I,H$. (One can weaken “uniform in $I$” to “for all but $o(1)$ of such intervals”, consistent with Maier-type irregularities.) This hypothesis implies both concentration of the prime count $N(I)$ around $T/\log x$ and Poisson-type statistics for rare local patterns such as prime pairs at separations $\le c$ inside a typical interval of length $T$. Take $T=A(\log x)^2$ with fixed $A>c_1$ and partition $[x/2,x]$ into disjoint intervals $I_j$ of length $T$. By the $m=1,2$ cases, $N(I_j)$ has mean $\sim A\log x$ and variance $O(\log x)$, so $N(I_j)\ge c_1\log x$ for all but $o(1)$ of the $I_j$ (Chebyshev). Let $B(I_j)$ be the number of prime pairs $(p,q)\subset I_j$ with $0<q-p\le c$. Using the fixed-$m$ cases of the conjecture and inclusion--exclusion/moment methods (à la Gallagher), one gets that $B(I_j)$ converges in distribution to $\mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda)$ with $\lambda=A\sum_{h=1}^c \mathfrak S(\{0,h\})$, hence $\mathbb P(B(I_j)=0)\to e^{-\lambda}>0$ for a positive proportion of intervals. Therefore some $I_j$ simultaneously satisfies $N(I_j)\ge c_1\log x$ and $B(I_j)=0$; the primes in that interval are automatically consecutive in the global sequence and, since there is no pair within distance $\le c$, every adjacent prime gap inside the block exceeds $c$, yielding $>c_1\log x$ consecutive primes $\le x$ with all pairwise differences $>c_2$.

"This is well-known if $c_1$ is sufficiently small." Does this mean that $\exists \epsilon, \forall 0 < c_1<\epsilon, \forall 0 < c_2, ...$ or $\forall 0 < c_2, \exists \epsilon, \forall 0 < c_1 <\epsilon...$? The first one is clearly stronger becauce $\epsilon$ does not depend on $c_2$.
I undertand It as the first option, or Else Erdos would likely had use Epsilon notation Instead of $c$'s.

I think only the weaker second formulation is easy to establish. (There are $\sim x/\log x$ prime gaps in $[x/2,x]$, of which $O(c_2 x/\log^2 x)$ of them are of size $\leq c_2$ by standard sieve theory bounds; hence, by the pigeonhole principle, one can find a sequence of $\gg \frac{1}{c_2} \log x$ consecutive gaps in $[x,2x]$ of size $> c_2$, giving the second form (in fact one can take $c_1$ proportional to $1/c_2$.)) The stronger first version might still be true (it seems plausible from standard probabilistic heuristics, which in fact predict consecutive runs as large as $\gg_{c_2} \log x \log\log x$), but looks much harder to establish unconditionally.

I am asking because I want to formalize this in Lean. According to [Er55c], the following two references may give an answer to my confusion: 1. P.Erdos, Acta Szeged, 15 (1949), 57-63. 2. M. Cugiani Annali di Matomatica Serie IV, 38 (1955), 309-320. But I am having difficulty finding them.
The first paper is here. The second is here.
Erdos proved the second formulation $∀0<c_2,∃ϵ,∀0<c1<ϵ...$ in theorem 3 of the first reference.
